title: Concrete will not be green
summary: An essay arguing that the search for a clean substitute for concrete is, for most infrastructure, a distraction from the harder and more important work of making concrete progressively less polluting.
body:
Concrete is the most-used material on the planet, after water. It is in our bridges, our flats, our hospitals, our seawalls. It is also, on most accounting, an environmental catastrophe. Producing one tonne of cement — the binding ingredient in concrete — releases roughly nine hundred kilograms of carbon dioxide. The cement industry is responsible for about eight per cent of global emissions, more than aviation and shipping combined.

It is therefore tempting to conclude that concrete is a problem to be replaced. A growing market in plant-based binders, mycelium bricks, and 'mass timber' construction is built on that hope. The argument has merit, especially for low-rise buildings. But the argument has a quiet asterisk that needs more attention than it usually receives.

For most twentieth-century infrastructure — multi-storey blocks, raised motorways, tunnels under cities, foundations that sit below the water table — there is, at present, no available substitute that performs the same job at the same scale. Wood does not behave like concrete in a fire. Mycelium does not span the gap between two motorway columns. The honest version of the green argument therefore must distinguish between two things: replacing concrete where alternatives now exist, and reducing the carbon cost of concrete in the places where it does not yet have one.

The latter is less photogenic and more important. Several promising approaches exist. Limestone calcined clay cement (LC3) replaces part of the conventional binder with locally sourced clay, reducing emissions by up to forty per cent without changing how the material is poured or how it cures. Carbon-injected concrete, developed in the early 2010s, traps small amounts of CO2 inside the concrete during mixing, mineralising it permanently. Some Indian and Brazilian plants now use industrial by-products — fly ash, blast-furnace slag — to substitute up to half the cement content.

None of these will make concrete 'green' in the sense the word is usually marketed. They make it less catastrophic. The distinction matters because the appetite for a clean substitute often distracts attention from the harder, less visible work of incrementally reforming an industry that — for the foreseeable future — is going to keep mixing, pouring, and curing.

There is a deeper objection one should acknowledge. Mainstream green campaigners are not wrong to worry that incremental approaches let polluting industries claim virtue cheaply. A 'low-carbon' cement is still emitting. The risk of greenwashing is real. The answer, in honest engineering circles, is to publish the emissions data per cubic metre and let architects, contractors, and city planners actually choose between options. The data is not yet universally available. It should be.

Concrete is not green. It is unlikely ever to be. The achievable goal — and the one that would matter most in terms of carbon — is concrete that is, year by year, less black.

annotations.meaningVi: xi măng (chất kết dính trong bê tông)
sợi nấm / hệ sợi nấm (vật liệu sinh học)
đã nung (xử lý nhiệt ở nhiệt độ cao)
tẩy xanh — quảng bá sai về tính thân thiện môi trường

grammarNotes.title: Concessive moves: granting before pressing the counter-argument
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Trong văn nghị luận B2, một bước quan trọng là 'nhượng bộ' (concession) trước khi phản biện — thừa nhận điểm mạnh của phía kia làm cho phản biện của mình đáng tin hơn. Trong bài: 'The argument has merit, especially for low-rise buildings.' (thừa nhận) → 'But the argument has a quiet asterisk…' (rồi mới chuyển hướng). 'Mainstream green campaigners are not wrong to worry…' (thừa nhận tinh tế bằng 'not wrong to') → 'The answer, in honest engineering circles…' (phản biện). Các cụm 'there is a deeper objection one should acknowledge' / 'one should acknowledge' / 'though' / 'has merit' đều là dấu hiệu của phép nhượng bộ.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: B2 argumentative writing often concedes ground before pushing back — granting the other side's strongest point makes your counter more credible. The passage uses 'The argument has merit, especially for low-rise buildings.' (concession) → 'But the argument has a quiet asterisk…' (turn). 'Mainstream green campaigners are not wrong to worry…' (subtle concession with 'not wrong to') → answer follows. Phrases like 'has merit', 'one should acknowledge', 'not wrong to', 'though' all signal concession before the counter.

translationVi:
Bê tông là vật liệu được dùng nhiều nhất hành tinh, chỉ sau nước. Nó có trong cầu, căn hộ, bệnh viện, kè chắn sóng của chúng ta. Nó cũng, theo phần lớn cách tính, là một thảm hoạ môi trường. Sản xuất một tấn xi măng — thành phần kết dính trong bê tông — thải ra khoảng chín trăm kg CO2. Ngành xi măng chịu trách nhiệm cho khoảng tám phần trăm lượng khí thải toàn cầu, nhiều hơn tổng hàng không và hàng hải cộng lại.

Vì vậy, dễ kết luận rằng bê tông là vấn đề cần thay thế. Một thị trường đang lên gồm chất kết dính từ thực vật, gạch mycelium, và xây dựng 'gỗ khối' được xây trên hi vọng đó. Lập luận có giá trị, nhất là với nhà thấp tầng. Nhưng lập luận có một dấu hoa thị lặng lẽ cần chú ý nhiều hơn nó thường được.

Với phần lớn hạ tầng thế kỷ XX — khối nhiều tầng, đường cao tốc trên cao, đường hầm dưới thành phố, móng nằm dưới mực nước ngầm — hiện chưa có vật liệu thay thế nào cùng làm được công việc đó ở cùng quy mô. Gỗ không cư xử như bê tông trong đám cháy. Mycelium không bắc qua khoảng giữa hai trụ cao tốc. Phiên bản trung thực của lập luận xanh do đó phải phân biệt hai điều: thay thế bê tông ở nơi đã có giải pháp khác, và giảm chi phí carbon của bê tông ở nơi chưa có.

Vế thứ hai ít ăn ảnh hơn nhưng quan trọng hơn. Có vài hướng đi triển vọng. Xi măng LC3 (Limestone Calcined Clay Cement) thay một phần chất kết dính truyền thống bằng đất sét tại chỗ, giảm phát thải tới bốn mươi phần trăm mà không đổi cách đổ và bảo dưỡng. Bê tông tiêm carbon, phát triển đầu thập niên 2010, giữ một lượng nhỏ CO2 trong bê tông trong khi trộn, biến nó thành khoáng vĩnh viễn. Một số nhà máy ở Ấn Độ và Brazil hiện dùng phụ phẩm công nghiệp — tro bay, xỉ lò cao — thay đến một nửa lượng xi măng.

Không có cái nào sẽ làm bê tông 'xanh' theo nghĩa từ này thường được tiếp thị. Chúng làm nó bớt thảm hoạ hơn. Sự phân biệt quan trọng vì cơn thèm một chất thay thế sạch hay làm xao nhãng khỏi công việc khó hơn, ít hấp dẫn về thị giác hơn: cải tổ dần một ngành mà — trong tương lai gần — sẽ còn tiếp tục trộn, đổ và bảo dưỡng.

Có một phản đối sâu hơn cần thừa nhận. Những nhà vận động môi trường chính thống không sai khi lo rằng cách tiếp cận tiệm tiến cho phép các ngành ô nhiễm tuyên bố đức hạnh với giá rẻ. Một 'xi măng phát thải thấp' vẫn đang phát thải. Nguy cơ 'tẩy xanh' là thật. Câu trả lời, trong các vòng kỹ sư trung thực, là công bố dữ liệu phát thải trên mỗi mét khối và để kiến trúc sư, nhà thầu, và nhà quy hoạch đô thị thật sự chọn giữa các phương án. Dữ liệu đó chưa được công khai phổ quát. Nó cần phải được.

Bê tông không xanh. Có lẽ sẽ không bao giờ xanh. Mục tiêu khả thi — và là mục tiêu quan trọng nhất xét về carbon — là một loại bê tông mà mỗi năm, ngày càng bớt đen.

questions.prompt: What is concrete's rank among the world's most-used materials?
questions.explanation: 'Concrete is the most-used material on the planet, after water.'

questions.prompt: Roughly how much CO2 does producing one tonne of cement release?
questions.explanation: 'Producing one tonne of cement… releases roughly nine hundred kilograms of carbon dioxide.'

questions.prompt: What percentage of global emissions comes from the cement industry, approximately?
questions.explanation: 'About eight per cent of global emissions.'

questions.prompt: What does the writer say the green substitute argument has merit for?
questions.explanation: 'The argument has merit, especially for low-rise buildings.'

questions.prompt: For which infrastructure does the writer say there is NO available substitute at scale?
options: Multi-storey blocks, raised motorways, tunnels, foundations below the water table

questions.prompt: Which two things must the honest version of the green argument distinguish between?
options: Replacing concrete where alternatives exist, and reducing the carbon cost of concrete where they do not

questions.prompt: By how much can LC3 cement reduce emissions, according to the passage?
questions.explanation: 'Reducing emissions by up to forty per cent.'

options: Traps small amounts of CO2 inside the concrete during mixing, mineralising it permanently
Removes all CO2 from the atmosphere
questions.explanation: 'Carbon-injected concrete, developed in the early 2010s, traps small amounts of CO2 inside the concrete during mixing, mineralising it permanently.'

questions.prompt: What industrial by-products are used by some Indian and Brazilian plants?
options: Fly ash and blast-furnace slag, replacing up to half the cement content
questions.explanation: 'Some Indian and Brazilian plants now use industrial by-products — fly ash, blast-furnace slag — to substitute up to half the cement content.'

options: No — they make it less catastrophic, not green in the marketed sense.
questions.explanation: 'None of these will make concrete "green" in the sense the word is usually marketed. They make it less catastrophic.'

questions.prompt: Why does the writer say the appetite for a clean substitute is dangerous?
options: It distracts attention from the harder, less visible work of reforming an industry that will continue mixing, pouring, and curing for the foreseeable future.

questions.prompt: How does the writer address the greenwashing objection?
options: By acknowledging it ('the risk is real') and proposing publication of per-cubic-metre emissions data
By calling for a return to wood
questions.explanation: Paragraph 6 contains the concession and the response.

questions.prompt: What concession does the writer make to mainstream green campaigners?
options: They are 'not wrong to worry' that incremental approaches let polluting industries claim virtue cheaply.
questions.explanation: 'Mainstream green campaigners are not wrong to worry that incremental approaches let polluting industries claim virtue cheaply.'

questions.prompt: What is the writer's final, achievable goal?
options: Concrete that is green within a year
Concrete that, year by year, is less black (not green, but progressively less catastrophic)
questions.explanation: 'Concrete is not green. It is unlikely ever to be. The achievable goal… is concrete that is, year by year, less black.'

questions.prompt: What is the main idea of the essay?
options: For most existing infrastructure, the realistic, important work is incremental decarbonisation of concrete, not replacement — and honest measurement, not marketed greenness, is the right yardstick.
Wood is the only sustainable material.
Climate change cannot be addressed at all.
questions.explanation: The essay defends an incremental, measurement-based approach against a substitution-first narrative.
questions.hint: Connect 'less black' with the data transparency argument.

cloze.template:
Concrete is the most-used material on the planet, after {{b1}}. It is in our bridges, our flats, our hospitals, our seawalls. It is also, on most accounting, an environmental {{b2}}. Producing one tonne of cement — the binding ingredient in concrete — releases roughly nine hundred kilograms of carbon dioxide. The cement industry is responsible for about {{b3}} per cent of global emissions, more than aviation and shipping combined.

It is therefore tempting to conclude that concrete is a problem to be {{b4}}. A growing market in plant-based binders, mycelium bricks, and 'mass timber' construction is built on that hope. The argument has merit, especially for {{b5}} buildings. But the argument has a quiet asterisk that needs more attention than it usually receives.

For most twentieth-century infrastructure — multi-storey blocks, raised motorways, tunnels under cities, foundations that sit below the water table — there is, at present, no available {{b6}} that performs the same job at the same scale. Wood does not behave like concrete in a fire. Mycelium does not span the gap between two motorway columns. The honest version of the green argument therefore must distinguish between two things: replacing concrete where alternatives now exist, and reducing the {{b7}} cost of concrete in the places where it does not yet have one.

The latter is less photogenic and more important. Several promising approaches exist. Limestone calcined clay cement (LC3) replaces part of the conventional binder with locally sourced {{b8}}, reducing emissions by up to forty per cent without changing how the material is poured or how it cures. Carbon-injected concrete, developed in the early 2010s, traps small amounts of CO2 inside the concrete during mixing, mineralising it permanently. Some Indian and Brazilian plants now use industrial by-{{b9}} — fly ash, blast-furnace slag — to substitute up to half the cement content.

None of these will make concrete 'green' in the sense the word is usually marketed. They make it less {{b10}}. The distinction matters because the appetite for a clean substitute often distracts attention from the harder, less visible work of incrementally reforming an industry that — for the foreseeable future — is going to keep mixing, pouring, and {{b11}}.

There is a deeper objection one should acknowledge. Mainstream green campaigners are not wrong to worry that incremental approaches let polluting industries claim virtue cheaply. A 'low-carbon' cement is still emitting. The risk of {{b12}} is real. The answer, in honest engineering circles, is to publish the emissions data per cubic {{b13}} and let architects, contractors, and city planners actually choose between options. The data is not yet universally available. It should be.

Concrete is not green. It is unlikely ever to be. The achievable goal — and the one that would matter most in terms of carbon — is concrete that is, year by year, less {{b14}}. The point of the essay, in the end, is not to defend concrete. It is to defend the {{b15}} measurement of it.

criticalThinkingQuestion: The essay argues that the 'clean substitute' narrative can distract from the harder work of partial reform. Is there an area of your own life — diet, transport, consumption — where you have spent more energy looking for a clean replacement than on incrementally reducing the harm of what you already do? Which would actually achieve more?
